So right in the southwest of Iran, Iranians are saying that the U.S. targeted that overnight.

We know that Qeshm Island, we know that Bandar Abbas in Iran were targets of U.S. Air Force overnight trying to get missile storage sites, drone storage sites, maritime sort of communication nodes along the Strait of Hormuz. But this morning as well, the Iranians targeting Kuwait again, targeting Bahrain again, the warning sirens going off in both of those countries.

So at the moment, this threat in Jordan, of course, that's the country where two U.S. service personnel, CENTCOM announced yesterday had been sadly killed in an Iranian strike at -- their one person, one service personnel. A person still missing in that attack. Four injured taken to hospital now released from hospital in Jordan. But it really does put Jordan very much in the crosshairs of Iran's actions against the U.S. interests in this region right now.

FREEMAN: Yes. Well said. All eyes on Jordan as this conflict again seems to be intensifying this weekend.

Nic Robertson, thank you very much. Appreciate your reporting.

Switching gears now, we have new details that are emerging about an ICE officer connected to the fatal shooting of a man in Maine. And we want to warn you, some of the video in this report may be disturbing. Now, CNN has confirmed that David Brouillette was among the officers at the scene. But CNN has not independently confirmed that Brouillette was the one who discharged his weapon.

CNN investigation has uncovered court records showing Brouillette previously reported lingering cognitive issues after a serious head injury and revealing other questions about his background. CNN's Gloria Pazmino takes a closer look.

GLORIA PAZMINO, CNN NATIONAL CORRESPONDENT: That's right. And we're getting a much fuller picture about who this officer is now that we have confirmed that David Brouillette was among the officers in the scene in Biddeford, Maine, where an ICE officer fatally shot, Joan Sebastian Duran Guerrero. This is all according to two of his ex-wives who reviewed photos and spoke with CNN.

Now, we should mention that CNN has not independently confirmed whether Brouillette was the officer who discharged the weapon killing Duran Guerrero. And investigators have not publicly identified the officer who shot the victim. Federal sources have separately confirmed to CNN that Brouillette is an ICE officer. The Department of Homeland Security has declined to comment on the identity of the officer, and they have said that the officer used deadly force because they feared for public safety.

Now, we have been trying to gather details to get a fuller picture about who Brouillette is. My colleagues Michael Williams, Kurt Devine and Priscilla Alvarez have been combing through public documents. And what has emerged is a troubling picture that raises serious questions about how ICE is doing its training and its officer vetting as they race to meet the demands of the Trump administration's immigration crackdown.

One of Brouillette's ex-wives and one of his daughters also told CNN that he contacted them days after the shooting and said that he had opened fire. Both said he told them that he thought the shooting was justified.

Now, here's some of what we have learned. In 2023, Brouillette told a court in Maine that he had suffered lingering cognitive issues after he suffered a serious head injury when a steel beam fell on his head during a training exercise for a firefighting certificate. He sued the Maine Community College system because of that injury, but it was just two years after he filed that lawsuit that he started working for ICE.

Brouillette's ex-wives also described a history of alleged domestic abuse and intimidation, all of this in court filings, where he denies the allegations. And we have not been able to confirm any criminal convictions or charges. We also reached out to Brouillette and he did not respond to several requests for comments.

We should mention Duran Guerrero's killing was the second time that an ICE officer shot and killed an immigrant behind the wheel in the span of just a week. And it's the fourth killing this year alone by federal immigration authorities.

Gloria Pazmino, CNN, New York.

FREEMAN: All right, Gloria, thank you for that reporting. All right. Still ahead, a brutal night across Ukraine as Russia unleashed its biggest missile attacks of the war. We have details ahead.

Plus, Taylor Farms is expanding its recall of iceberg linked to diarrhea outbreak. We'll tell you which products to look out for.

And let's take a live look now at East Rutherford, New Jersey, where just hours from now, Argentina and Spain are going to battle it out in the World Cup final. We're going to show you what to expect after a quick break.

FREEMAN: The time has come. After more than 100 matches, two teams are going to battle it out in today's FIFA World Cup final. Spain versus Argentina, and two soccer giants will meet on the pitch. Argentina's Lionel Messi versus Spain's Lamine Yamal.

Leigh Waldman is live outside of the New York-New Jersey stadium where these games are taking place. Leigh, the excitement is palpable. What are we expecting today?

LEIGH WALDMAN, CNN CORRESPONDENT: Danny, so much excitement in the air. We just heard them testing the mic system inside and, you know, it gets your heart pumping. We're ready to see these two teams face off.

And they play very different styles on the pitch, Spain and Argentina. People are comparing it to orchestra versus heavy metal. We know that Argentina will be very aggressive on the pitch.

Meanwhile, Spain is hoping to play a complex passing game and take any advantage that they can to try and score on Argentina to make this match even more special. It's the very first time in a World Cup final we are seeing it contested by the champions of the world versus the champions of England. So many fans coming here trying to take in a moment out of what is going to be an incredible match here, Danny.

FREEMAN: I'm curious, I know that President Trump is expected to attend today's game. Can you take us through what that means for security for this final?

WALDMAN: Absolutely. So we have heard from FIFA officials and they say they are heavily increasing the security measures that are in place here at the stadium behind us. Not only because of President Donald Trump, but multiple other international dignitaries who are expected to be in attendance today.

This is a SEAR 1 event. That is the highest level of security coordinated by the FBI, DHS and Secret Service. They're right now are putting in active roadblocks to shut down the traffic around this stadium. Fans who are in attendance today, they can expect to go through extensive TSA style screenings in order to get inside of this stadium.

It's a no drone zone. If you're coming to this game today, you're being told come hours early ahead of that 3:00 p.m. kickoff.

FREEMAN: Wow, that's wild. Meanwhile, Leigh, New York I know has been dealing with a lot of poor air quality conditions from those Canadian wildfires. We were looking at a live picture of the stadium a moment ago.

Still seems a little hazy out there. How is it out there? Is it going to affect the game today? What do you think?

WALDMAN: Just a little bit hazy. But, Danny, we never cheer for weekend rain showers, right? Yesterday was a rainy day in New York City. But that's actually helping with the wildfire smoke that we've been dealing with these last few days.

Last week, very hazy, smelled like a campfire all throughout New York City. Right now, this is a lot better. It's going to be humid. There's still going to be a little bit of smoke in the air. But 80 degrees today when these two teams kick off. So it's going to be a much better day for the players on the pitch but also for the fans who are here to attend.

FREEMAN: All right. Last thing, Leigh. We can't talk about the final without talking about ticket prices. How much is it going to cost for last-minute tickets? Should we get one? Should we get two tickets and go?

WALDMAN: OK, Danny. So I'm going to say our DMs are open. So if anyone is feeling generous and wants to send us some tickets, we can go together. But unless you've been saving up probably your entire life, I don't think you're affording a ticket inside today.

The cheapest tickets, Joey, my producer, and I were looking this morning, almost $8,000. And that's for a nosebleed seat. If you want to get closer to the action, you're shelling out $20,000, $30,000, even $40,000 for one of these tickets. But they're also offering some bundle packages to include your food and drinks.

Who doesn't want that? $32,000 to $33,000 there. This is an expensive game to go ahead and see. All those fans who are flying into the United States, I don't know where they're getting all this money, man.

FREEMAN: Listen, $32,000, I should hope that you could get at least a hot dog included with that.

Leigh Waldman, thank you so much. Great to see you. Have a great time out there. Looking forward to it.

All right, to this now. After days of extreme heat and smoke, New Yorkers found themselves actually dealing with a completely different problem, flash flooding. Heavy rain moved through the region yesterday, flooding roads and causing problems on parts of the transit system.

Some neighborhoods actually picked up several inches of rain in just a few hours. Lower Manhattan, along with parts of Brooklyn and Queens, took the hardest hit. And officials urged people living in basement apartments to move to higher floors.

CNN's Melissa Nord joins me now. Melissa, again, the thunderstorms were intense. The lightning, the rain. What can people expect today, though?

MELISSA NORD, CNN METEOROLOGIST: Well, they came down really fast and heavy, Danny. Some of those rainfall amounts in just a matter of 30 minutes. Some places picked up an inch, an inch and a half of rainfall. Record rainfall yesterday.

JFK, Newark, Manhattan picked up over three inches. Dozens of flooding reports there. But what's good about that system coming yesterday is that we got the rain out of here and the smoke out of here. The front's to the south now. All the wildfire smoke infiltrating the Midwest. So air quality today looking much better at the stadium.

Three o'clock kickoff will be in the low 80s. And humidity is also lower, making it a lot more comfortable outside. But as that front sags a little bit south, two areas of concern for severe weather, the first of which over the Carolinas, level two risk of severe weather, damaging winds, even an isolated tornado can't be ruled out.

That chance of damaging wind stretches into Atlanta. We'll get that daytime heating going. That helps to bring in a new round of heavy thunderstorms. Some of those could lead to flash flooding and then to the south of their trouble in the tropics as well.

This area in the eastern Gulf now has a 60 percent chance of development. The hurricane hunters with NOAA going to be flying into the system in this 91 L to investigate what the winds looking like, regardless of development or not, it's a rainmaker just along the coastline from the Big Bend Apalachicola down to Tampa.

We'll see some rounds of heavy rain moving on shore that could lead to some isolated accumulations. That could be 4 inches with some flash flooding. Danny?

FREEMAN: Melissa Nord, thank you so much for that forecast. Appreciate it.

All right. Coming up in just a moment, a duo of controversial influencers is back in legal trouble. The Tate brothers have been arrested in the U.S. and are facing extradition to the U.K. We're going to explain the accusations against them.

Plus, the U.S. military carries out another round of strikes on Iran as details of the deaths of two U.S. service members are revealed. Stay with us.

FREEMAN: This just in. The Jordanian government is now denying there were evacuations of air or sea ports there. And this comes after the U.S. embassy said they were evacuated due to a credible threat.

The U.S. has now bombed Iran for an eighth straight night. Now targeting facilities for air and coastal defenses and weapons storage sites, according to U.S. Central Command. And two American soldiers were killed following Iranian strikes in Jordan Friday and one is still missing.

Joining me now is Major John Spencer, chair of War Studies for the Madison Policy Forum. He's also the author of the upcoming book, "20 Times I Almost Died." Major Spencer, thank you so much for joining us this morning on a hard weekend for our service members.

Let's start there, though. We still don't have a lot of information about these attacks in Jordan that killed those two service members. But what's your perspective here? What do you think this means for next steps in this war?

MAJOR JOHN SPENCER (RET,), CHAIR OF WAR STUDIES, MADISON POLICY FORUM: I think that it means that, one, we're going to mourn our losses but understand that the greatest thing we can do, in my opinion, to our service members is to accomplish the mission and finish the job. We have an integrated air defense system on all of our bases, but Iran's continuing to attack all of the countries, I think seven countries in seven days, where U.S. forces or not, because they're attacking desalination plants in Kuwait and other locations, striking back at the U.S. attacks now on eight consecutive nights.

FREEMAN: What do you believe this says about the vulnerability of our troops or our military installations in the region right now?

SPENCER: I mean, I think we have very strong defenses, but there is no perfect system, even what we've seen in Israel that has, you know, its Iron Dome and other systems. If you can still get about 1 percent, and that's what Iran is getting, depending on where we're talking about, that's still something's going to get through and adjustments are made, and they've been using different types of tactics, you know, different missiles of the capabilities they have left.

FREEMAN: Major, I'm curious, from your perspective, is the U.S. right now underestimating the capabilities of Iran and these proxies in the region? Because we've been told time and time again that their military has been seriously weakened, and yet we see instances like this.

SPENCER: No, I don't think we underestimate them, the U.S. military, and I think we're moving more assets into the region to stop their ability to influence the Strait of Hormuz. That's what we're really seeing now is the Battle of Hormuz being fought through and U.S. forces in different areas of these countries. Of course, they're in harm's way, right? That's what our troops are signed up for, but I do think we're underestimating them. And even if you destroyed a vast majority of their capabilities, they still have capabilities. It's a large country that was invested in these capabilities, drones, missiles, for a long time to be able to do just this.

So I don't think it's underestimating. I mean, it's the will that is the problem at this point, not the capability, although we don't like seeing any capability being projected out of Iran, but that's the importance of the mission is that this is about changing the regime's behavior, not a regime change operation, and they're vastly different.

FREEMAN: Major, from your view, are we closer to peace today? I mean, it seemed like for a moment there, there were signings of MOUs, there were steps towards ceasefires, and then it seems like in the past week plus or so, certainly this weekend, we've taken several steps backwards.

SPENCER: Yes, I think we all had a -- or had a hope that the memorandum of understanding, which is an agreement to have a conversation, would lead to a negotiated settlement of the operation. I mean, I don't think we're closer now, of course.

According to President Trump, the ceasefire is over. The MOU, despite both sides maybe thinking that it said something different in -- especially in relation to the Strait of Hormuz, I don't think we're closer to peace at this moment and that we will likely have to accelerate the operation in order to achieve that goal of getting to the negotiation table.

It's always going to end at a negotiation table, but I do believe that the region and the United States is safer today. They're not closer to a nuclear weapon. Their capabilities to project power have been drastically impacted.

FREEMAN: Major, the Pentagon has mostly stopped doing media briefings now and updates regularly on the war, so reporters have not really been able to ask questions of the administration about the latest objectives or where things stand. I'm just curious, what do you make of that? Do you think that's an important thing that should be happening more frequently?

SPENCER: Absolutely. I'm definitely a big fan of communicating at all levels, so both from the White House Press Corps Secretary and the Pentagon. But I think we're in this situation where they do want the negotiations to continue, and according to President Trump and others, that they are talking to people in Iran, and people want that to work without the statements, even though the new supreme leader made a statement, although nobody's seen him, on what he thinks about the negotiation and memorandum understanding. I think we're in a sensitive situation, and hopefully we do see those briefings, because I do think they're a daily to have them, is important.

DANNY FREEMAN, CNN ANCHOR: And last question for me, Major, is, I know it's hard to project into the future, especially since we're only still a few months into this conflict, but is this looking more and more like a forever war to you?

SPENCER: No, not at all. And I use that term very, I don't use that often, because I understand this isn't a forever war, a nation- building war, we're going to overthrow the regime and institute a democracy. This is a limited war. Four limited objectives, I think, are worth fighting for, in my opinion, non-nuclear proliferation and the freedom of navigation, which would impact the U.S. economy from the gas prices to fertilizer across the globe if Iran was allowed to do what they are trying to say that they want to do, which is control the Strait of Hormuz.

I think it's worth fighting for and achieving this limited goal, but not a forever war. And although we are, you know, whether you count the entire time, five months into it, that there has been progress made. And I do believe that there are people willing to come to terms, but it has to be on these very non-negotiable aspects of no nuclear weapons and freedom of navigation.

FREEMAN: Major John Spencer, thank you very much for your insight and perspective this morning. Really do appreciate it.

SPENCER: Thanks, Danny.

FREEMAN: All right. To this now, Russia just unleashed a massive ballistic missile attack against Ukraine, one of the largest since the war began over four years ago. More than 40 missiles and 120 drones were fired overnight by the Russians, most of them targeting the capital of Kyiv. Rescue operations are underway at this hour. And the attack follows deadly strikes by the Ukrainians deep into Russia, destroying two e-commerce warehouses, one of them in Moscow. At least eight people were killed there.

CNN's Barbie Latza Nadeau has been tracking the latest developments. Barbie, this war really seems to be escalating at a dangerous pace at this point.

BARBIE LATZA NADEAU, CNN CONTRIBUTOR: Yes. You know, this was really a brutal attack against Ukrainian capital. Forty of those missiles raining down in less than two hours, sparking fires in residential areas and in a shopping area and in a mall. But this does come on the heels of those twin drone attacks deep into Russia, including one of these centers near Moscow.

Now, eight people were killed in that attack. And the Ukraine says that those were logistical centers that were used in drone production and drone. Let's listen to what President Volodymyr Zelenskyy had to say about it.

VOLODYMYR ZELENSKYY, UKRAINIAN PRESIDENT (through translator): These were Russian logistics facilities, including warehouses supplying equipment for the war effort, such as navigation systems, components used in drone production and other supplies for the Russian military. Our Ukrainian weapons also struck another oil facility and targets in the waters of the Sea of Azov and the Black Sea. This was an important operation. There will be more.

NADEAU: And what's really important about this, and I think something to understand, is this is a change of strategy for Ukraine. Previously, they had only attacked energy infrastructure and maritime assets. To go so deep into the country and attack an area that is for e-commerce, you know, distribution is a change.

Now, whether or not that is going to change Russia's approach is unknown yet. And whether it's going to bring them closer to the negotiating table. Clearly, Russians saw the black pillowing smoke across their country on Saturday. Whether that's going to make any difference in the negotiation remains to be seen.

But all eyes here in Europe are definitely on what happens next, and if this escalation spreads beyond Ukraine. Danny.

FREEMAN: They certainly are. All right. Barbie Latza Nadeau, thank you very much for that reporting. Really do appreciate it.

All right. Let's check in on some other headlines this morning. We're learning more about the arrest of social media influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate. U.S. Marshals arrested the brothers yesterday in Miami on an extradition request from the U.K. British prosecutors accused the brothers of rape, sex trafficking, and other offenses tied to an investigation involving multiple alleged victims. They're expected to appear in federal court in Miami this week as the extradition process moves forward. The brothers have repeatedly denied wrongdoing.

And produce supplier, Taylor Farms, is recalling iceberg lettuce shipped to 27 states due to potential cyclospora contamination. This is the same company that supplied shredded lettuce to Taco Bell restaurants that have been linked to the cyclosporiasis outbreak in the Midwest. The recall covers lettuce distributed between June 29th and July 16th. If you had lettuce to Taco Bell restaurants that have been linked to the cyclosporiasis outbreak in the Midwest, the recall covers lettuce distributed between June 29th and July 16th. If you have it, the company says, you should throw it away immediately.

And protest against the building of data centers took place across the country on Saturday. A handful of people protested over a highway overpass in Berkeley, California. Opponents worry about noise, pollution, and strained resources. However, supporters argued these facilities are vital to expanding technology like artificial intelligence while boosting local economies.

Still ahead, Maine Democrats begin the process of replacing former Senate candidate, Graham Platner. We're going to dig into the race to the nomination.

And if you're heading out, remember, you can stream our show from anywhere in the U.S. right from the CNN app. You can also always go to cnn.com/watch. We'll be right back. (COMMERCIAL BREAK)

FREEMAN: Democrats in Maine have been scrambling to find a replacement for former U.S. Senate nominee Graham Platner and it looks like they may have found one. Troy Jackson dominated the first day of Maine's two-day delegate selection process on Saturday. The blue- collar logger and former state Senate president captured a large majority of the delegates with his progressive message. Take a listen.

TROY JACKSON (D), MAINE SENATE CANDIDATE: I believe that we can take on corporate price gouging and lower costs for working families. I believe we can build housing that people can afford. I believe that workers deserve the freedom to unionize without intimidation, retaliation, or fear.

FREEMAN: For more on this, let's bring in Michael Shepherd. He's a reporter with the Banger Daily News. Michael, thank you so much for getting up early and chatting with us. Let's start here. Is this race looking competitive anymore or is Jackson actually in a super strong position after this weekend?

MICHAEL SHEPHERD, POLITICS EDITOR AND REPORTER, BANGOR DAILY NEWS: No, it's not looking competitive. He won 290 by my count of the first 319 delegates that were decided on Saturday. We'll have the rest awarded in county caucuses today and this is to a state convention that happens next weekend in Bangor. So, that was a dominant share by any measure. He's now, again by my count, which I think is conservative, only 11 delegates shy of hitting that 301.

Now, it's worth pointing out that these delegates are formally unbound, but these are people who are on his slate or have told me or someone else that they're supporting him. So, he's in a really, really commanding position. I would be surprised if his opponents, if this goes the way it went yesterday, are in the race after today.

FREEMAN: It's remarkable how quickly all of this really came together after Platner's exit. I'm curious though, will Jackson's former support of Platner weigh him down at all if he advances to the general? What do you think?

SHEPHERD: Yes, this is a tough balance for him to strike in this race. He is the closest thing to a one-to-one comparison to Graham Platner. Both are deeply tied to organized labor. Platner ranked Jackson first on his gubernatorial ballot when he was in that primary back in June. Jackson was a really early kind of institutional endorser of Platner early in the race and had a rough relationship with Governor Janet Mills.

So, they are really, really closely tied. And all the candidates in this SNAP-nominating race have tried to hitch themselves to Platner's progressive policies and his sort of organizing momentum. I think Jackson picked up a lot of the support from the people on the ground who were really behind Platner. So, it's a delicate balance because these candidates want to bring in the progressive movement that Platner created but leave him beh

U.S. Wraps Up Eighth Night Of Strikes Against Iran; Two U.S. Service Members Dead; One Missing After Friday Attack; Kuwait Army Intercepting Missile, Drone Attacks From Iran; Deadly Maine Shooting Sparks Anti-ICE Protests; Ontario Premier Says It's A Shame Trump Is Blaming Canada For Wildfire Smoke; Flash Flooding Follows Days Of Smoke And Heat In New York; World Cup Fans Stranded As New York Storms Cause Airport Chaos; Spain And Argentina Prepare To Face Off In The World Cup Final; Tate Brothers Arrested In Miami On U.K. Extradition Request; Iceberg Lettuce Recalled In 27 States Due To Possible Contamination; Michigan Democrats Battle For Open Senate Seat; Russia Launches Massive Missile And Drone Attack Against Ukraine. Iran Suspending Commitment To Ceasefire Agreement; Top Two Teams In The World Make It To World Cup Finals; Catching Up With Quilen Blackwell, 2025 CNN Hero Of The Year; Andy Burnham To Take Over As U.K. Prime Minister Monday. Aired 6-7a ET

Aired July 19, 2026 - 06:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

DANNY FREEMAN, CNN ANCHOR: Welcome to CNN THIS MORNING. We're very glad you're with us here today.

Here's what's new this morning. First up, the U.S. launched another round of strikes on Iran, Saturday, in retaliation for the death of two U.S. soldiers. And happening now, we're learning of new missile attacks by Iran on U.S. allies. We've got that coming up in a live report.

And we're also following breaking news out of Ukraine. Russia is unleashing a major ballistic missile attack on Kyiv. We have new information and video of the damage coming in.

And here in the U.S. first it was the smoke. Well, now it was the floods. Part of the northeast are drying out after flash floods swept through the region. We've got a look at the damage there and today's forecast.

Plus, after more than 100 matches, thrilling wins and heartbreaking losses, it's all come down to this, the World Cup final. And let's take a live look at the stadium where Spain and Argentina are going to face off in just a little bit. We're going to take you there live coming up.

A very good morning to you. It is Sunday, July 19th. Thank you so much for joining us. I'm Danny Freeman in for Victor Blackwell.

And breaking overnight, U.S. ally Kuwait says it is actively intercepting missile and drone attacks, citing Iranian aggression. Sirens are also going off in Bahrain. Now, that happened just hours after U.S. Central Command posted this video saying it wrapped up an eighth night of strikes against Iran. CENTCOM says it targeted Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Forces behind attacks that killed two U.S. service members in Jordan on Friday. One service member is still missing.

For more on this, we have CNN's Nic Robertson joining us now. Nic, what more have we learned this morning?

NIC ROBERTSON, CNN INTERNATIONAL DIPLOMATIC EDITOR: Well, the very latest is the latest round of strikes from Iran on Bahrain that was ongoing literally an hour ago. And a couple of hours before that as you mentioned Kuwait was taking the force of Iran's attacks.

I think overall last night, it does appear Iran has targeted fewer countries in the Gulf region than it has done over previous nights. It's not clear if it targeted anywhere in Jordan, but Jordan has absolutely, over the past five or six days, been a real focus for Iranian strikes. They say that they've been hitting U.S. Air Force assets at different military bases inside of Jordan. We don't yet know the details about the strike that killed the two service members in Jordan, or the missing third member. No update on that person missing in action.

Those who have been injured, U.S. service personnel injured in that strike in Jordan have been released from hospital. But where does that leave the bigger picture in the region at the moment? One of the things that we're seeing is that the U.S. is increasing its fleet of the KC-135 Stratotankers, the air refuelers for the F-35 and other fighter jets that are critical to prosecuting the war against Iran in the region at the moment and relocating some of them to Israel.

So in that -- in this context where Iran is trying to hit U.S. military assets closer to Iran, putting these assets in Israel, potentially making them safer, harder for Iran to reach Israel has strong -- very strong defensive procedures against incoming Iranian missiles. It has not been targeted yet.

What the United States was targeting overnight were missile storage and drone storage facilities was targeting along the Strait of Hormuz. Again on the Island of Qeshm, which is right at that sort of critical, narrow point in the Strait of Hormuz and Bandar Abbas, which is the main city. Again, Iranian city on the Strait of Hormuz.

It's not clear how much this latest salvo of overnight operations from the United States has damaged Iran's ability to interdict shipping in the -- in the Strait of Hormuz. But that is -- that is the plan to stop Iran being able to cut off international maritime shipping in the strait.

FREEMAN: And again, still heartbreaking for the families of those service members who were killed. Nic Robertson, thank you so much for that latest update.

[06:05:01] Really do appreciate it. Switching gears now, we have new details about one of the officers on scene when 25-year-old Johan Sebastian Duran Guerrero was shot and killed in Maine back on Monday. Now a warning, the video you're about to see may be disturbing.

CNN now confirmed David Brouillette was among those officers, according to two of his ex-wives. One of those ex-wives and one of his daughters told CNN that he told them he opened fire and thought the shooting was justified. CNN has not independently confirmed if Brouillette was the officer who shot Duran Guerrero, and federal officials have not publicly identified that officer.

Now, after combing through public documents and speaking with those ex-wives, CNN uncovered more details about Brouillette's past. Both women described a history of alleged domestic abuse and intimidation. Brouillette has denied the allegations and was never charged.

Another note from his past, Brouillette told a court in Maine that he suffered lingering cognitive issues after he suffered a serious head injury. A steel beam fell on his head while he was in firefighter training, leaving him unable to complete that training. Well, Brouillette has not responded to several requests by CNN for comment. Now, the shooting has sparked protests across Maine like this.

UNIDENTIFIED FEMALE: I mean, I think just grief stricken silence, anger, disbelief, shock. Yes, because it happened here.

UNIDENTIFIED MALE: I was down at Biddeford and I'm here because I care about that. And, you know, I mean, who doesn't?

FREEMAN: Twenty-five year old Duran Guerrero was from Colombia. He was here with his partner and daughter working as a delivery driver. The Department of Homeland Security says he was trying to flee the scene, and an ICE officer shot him, quote, "fearing for public safety."

Also this morning, nearly 1,000 wildfires are burning across Canada. In Ontario alone, crews are battling 191 active fires, with more than 150 firefighting teams spread across the province.

Now the smoke is drifting deep into the U.S., Midwest and East Coast, prompting air quality alerts for more than 100 million people. President Trump is blaming Canada for the smoke and threatening to impose tariffs on Canada over it. Yesterday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford pushed back.

DOUG FORD, ONTARIO PREMIER: I find it a shame, the administration in the U.S. and Congress people, writing these letters and blaming us and so on and so forth. They have a very short, short memory. We had the water bombers, everyone ready, going over to California. It was last year. We sent down hydro linemen down to the Carolinas and Georgia to help out. That's what neighbors do, right? You don't get on and start threatening and criticizing because, guess what? One day it's going to be your turn.

FREEMAN: Meanwhile, thousands of people have been forced from their homes in Canada with no clear timeline for when they can return. And of course, here in the U.S., the smoke is disrupting everything from summer events to outdoor activities across more than a dozen states, including near the venue for today's World Cup final.

Though after days of smoke and extreme heat, New Yorkers found themselves dealing with a completely different problem, flash flooding. Heavy rain moved through the region yesterday, flooding roads and causing problems on parts of the transit system. Some areas picked up several inches of rain in just a few hours.

CNN's Melissa Nord joins me now. Melissa, I was, you know, I'm here in New York, and it really was coming down yesterday. What can people expect today?

MELISSA NORD, CNN METEOROLOGIST: I know it was really intense, but it's going to be looking a lot better, Danny, today in New York for that World Cup final. You can see over 400 reports of damaging winds yesterday. And there are all of those flash flooding reports through New York City.

The front, though is further south. So we've had this wind direction shift. It's pushed the smoke out of there. It is encompassing the Midwest today. But for New York itself, it looks great for game time.

We've got kickoff there at 3:00. We'll be in the 80s. There's low humidity. So really nice weather there.

The storm system that brought that flash flooding to New York City yesterday further south at this point, that's going to bring some severe weather to the Carolinas, down to Georgia. It's daytime heating. So it's driven in the afternoon and evening watch for heavy rain that could lead to flash flooding and damaging winds.

And then south of there, we're also watching some trouble in the tropics. This little swirl across the Eastern Gulf now has a 60 percent chance of development. Hurricane hunters expected to fly into this later today to get more information. It's going to swirl around in the Eastern Gulf the next couple of days, bringing some intense rain bands right along the coastline.

It could become a tropical depression if it becomes a named storm Bertha would be the name there. You can see forecast rainfall. Much of this just right along the Big Bend region, Apalachicola, and even down to the Tampa area. We could see locally some places get around four inches of rain, Danny.

FREEMAN: Man, a wet week coming up. All right. Melissa, thank you so much. Really do appreciate it. And severe weather has left soccer fans stranded just ahead of the World Cup final in New Jersey. The intense rain forced ground stops at JFK, LaGuardia, and Newark airports, triggering a cascade of cancellations and major flight delays for thousands of fans traveling on the eve of today's championship match.

BLAKE FERTITTA, FAN TRAVELING TO WORLD CUP FINAL: Yes, mostly been checking online and see that the ground stop has finally ended, so hopefully they'll board and we can get up there tonight. That's the hope. If not, either try to recoup the tickets on a -- you know, sell the ticket or potentially get a rental car through the night.

FREEMAN: I hope they get there. All right. Coming up next, we're going to take you live to New Jersey and the site of today's World Cup final, where fans are already making their predictions. And we're going to tell you what fans can expect if they're heading to the match as well.

Plus, also a duo of controversial influencers are back in legal trouble. They've been arrested in the U.S. and are facing extradition to the U.K. We're going to explain the accusations against the Tate brothers.

And later, Andy Burnham is set out to become the UK's new prime minister tomorrow. We're going to lay out the challenges he's facing as Britain's incoming new leader.

FREEMAN: After 38 days of nonstop action, the 2026 World Cup final is finally here. At 3:00 p.m. eastern today, world number one Argentina faces world number two Spain in front of a packed new stadium. It's going to be a spectacular clash featuring soccer superstar Lionel Messi and prodigy Lamine Yamal. President Trump will also be in attendance, and FIFA's president even stated that Trump would hand the trophy to the winning team.

Leigh Waldman is live outside of the New York -- New Jersey stadium where these games are taking place. Leigh, so great to see you and so much excitement ahead of today's match.

LEIGH WALDMAN, CNN CORRESPONDENT: Danny, so much excitement. The 3:00 a.m. alarm is not so bad on a Sunday when this is what your day at work is here. It has all led up to this moment. These two teams have such different playing styles on the pitch, so this is going to be a really thrilling match to watch between Spain and Argentina. But even if you don't necessarily identify as a sports fan, this day has something for everyone, including a performance from Miss FIFA herself, Shakira, Shakira.

GOV. MIKIE SHERRILL (D-NJ): Welcoming the world to New Jersey. Some people have compared it to having your cousins over and your mom let you stay up late.

WALDMAN (voice-over): An incredible tournament that has brought memorable moments on and off the field.

SHAUN ALEXANDER, WORLD CUP FAN VISITING U.S. FROM SCOTLAND: Buc-ee's is like America in a box, a very, very large box. But, you know, you go in there, it's very colorful. There's all these kind of gifts and all this different food and smells.

WALDMAN (voice-over): After weeks of intense matches, it all comes down to this. Two giants meeting on the pitch in New Jersey. La Roja versus La Albiceleste.

UNIDENTIFIED YOUNG FAN: I think they would be pretty proud of seeing all these fans just happy to support them always.

UNIDENTIFIED YOUNG FAN: It was really cool seeing Spain walk by, seeing all my favorite players walk by. Yes.

UNIDENTIFIED YOUNG FAN: All the players are great and it's like a great experience to see everybody. And Spain is going to win the World Cup.

WALDMAN (voice-over): This is the first time the two teams have played each other at the World Cup since the group stage of the 1966 tournament in England, when Argentina won two-one. While both teams have a lot riding on this game, Argentina is playing for history, trying to be the first repeat champions since the 1960s.

Today's final has something for everyone, including those who don't identify as sports fans. Ahead of the game, FIFA is hosting a star studded show with performances from some of the biggest names in music and entertainment. At halftime, Madonna, Justin Bieber, Shakira, BTS and more will be coming together for what's sure to be an unforgettable performance.

WALDMAN: And, Danny, if we need to make today even more exciting, let me add another level for you. This is the first ever World Cup final to be contested by the champions of the world versus the champions of Europe. Messi, he is hoping to answer a very important question today. Is he the GOAT?

FREEMAN: Yes, I think he's for sure the GOAT. That's undisputed. And, Leigh, I just love that you are wearing the blue and white stripes for Argentina. And also the red earrings for Spain. I really like that. Nice touch. Leigh Waldman, thanks so much. Have a good time out there. All right. Coming up after a break, we have new information about the cyclospora outbreak. The company behind the contaminated lettuce is issuing a recall. We're going to tell you what you need to know coming up next.

FREEMAN: In headlines this morning, we're learning more about the arrest of social media influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate. U.S. marshals arrested the brothers yesterday in Miami on an extradition request from the U.K. British prosecutors accused the brothers of rape, sex trafficking and other offenses tied to an investigation involving multiple alleged victims. They're expected to appear in federal court in Miami this week as the extradition process moves forward. The brothers have repeatedly denied wrongdoing.

And produce supplier Taylor Farms is recalling iceberg lettuce shipped to 27 states due to potential cyclospora contamination. This is the same company that supplied shredded lettuce to Taco Bell restaurants that have been linked to the cyclosporiasis outbreak in the Midwest. The recall covers lettuce distributed between June 29th and July 16th. If you have it, the company says you should immediately throw it away.

And later today, Michigan Democrats will get another look at one of the party's most closely watched Senate primaries. Abdul El-Sayed is set to rally with Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez in Grand Rapids. El-Sayed and Congresswoman Haley Stevens are competing to replace retiring Democratic Senator Gary Peters. As he campaigns for the Senate, El-Sayed is framing his message around who he says really holds power in Washington. Here's part of what he told supporters.

ABDUL EL-SAYED (D), MICHIGAN SENATE CANDIDATE: Donald Trump is not himself the disease of our politics.

He's just the worst symptom of the disease of our politics. The disease is the system that allows big corporations, billionaires, special interests like AIPAC to buy and sell, politicians to do their bidding instead of ours. We have to pay more for all we pay for. Get paid less for the work we do, and watch as our tax dollars get misappropriated to drop bombs and buy tanks abroad, rather than to invest in schools and health care here at home.

FREEMAN: And coming up later this morning, El-Sayed joins Jake Tapper on "STATE OF THE UNION." Moving abroad, Russia just launched one of its largest ballistic missile attacks against Ukraine since the war began over four years ago. Ukraine says more than 40 missiles and 120 drones were fired overnight by the Russians, most of them targeting the capital, Kyiv. Rescue operations are underway at this hour. The attacks followed deadly strikes by the Ukrainians deep into Russia, destroying two e-commerce warehouses, one of them in Moscow. At least eight people were killed.

PRESIDENT VOLODYMYR ZELENSKYY, UKRAINE (through translator): These were Russian logistics facilities, including warehouses supplying equipment for the war effort, such as navigation systems, components used in drone production and other supplies for the Russian military. Our Ukrainian weapons also struck another oil facility and targets in the waters of the Sea of Azov and the Black Sea. This was an important operation. There will be more.

FREEMAN: CNN's Barbie Latza Nadeau is tracking the latest developments. Barbie, this war really seems to be escalating to a dangerous new level.

BARBIE LATZA NADEAU, CNN CONTRIBUTOR: Yes, this really was a brutal overnight attack on the Ukrainian capital. Those 40 missiles raining down in less than two hours, sparking fires, residential areas were hit, a shopping center was hit. But of course, they are retaliating against that Ukrainian twin drone strike deep into the heart of Russia, very close to Moscow, hitting those distribution centers.

You know, this really is seeming to be an escalation of the war right now. And it remains to be seen if Ukraine is now going to or has the capability to strike Russia again inside, and whether or not this sort of escalation is going to bring either side closer to the negotiating table and bring these countries in this ongoing war closer to an end and peace in the region.

It has been four difficult years, obviously, in Ukraine. Reverberations all across Europe here with higher prices and Ukrainian refugees. So everybody's looking for an end to this, except it seems, Vladimir Putin and Russia -- Danny.

FREEMAN: All right. Barbie Latza Nadeau, thank you very much for that update and report.

All right. Let's get more now on our top story this morning. Two U.S. service members killed in action in Jordan, the first American fatalities from Iranian fire since March. And another U.S. soldier is missing. The U.S. retaliating an eighth straight night of strikes targeting Islamic Revolutionary Guard Forces believed to be behind those attacks.

CNN global affairs analyst Kimberly Dozier joins us now from Washington to break all this down. Kim, good to see you. Though I'm sad to see you on a hard weekend for the U.S.

Listen, we still don't have a ton of information about this attack in Jordan that killed those two service members. But what's your read on where this conflict stands today after this awful news? I mean, surely this will only make things worse

KIMBERLY DOZIER, CNN GLOBAL AFFAIRS ANALYST: Yes. First of all, we don't have much information because the way the U.S. military does its notification, it only does it between 6:00 a.m. and 10:00 p.m. in wherever the families are located, so that they never have to fear a knock at the door in the middle of the night with the worst possible news of their lives. So that process is ongoing, and then we'll likely get more details about how those troops were lost after that notification.

As for the battle itself between the U.S. and Iran, what this shows is that while the U.S. might have taken out Iran's conventional military capability, largely that Iran maintains weapons and militia groups and operatives throughout the region that have been there for years, and they still have capability. And so wherever the U.S. has a weak point, troops in a vulnerable location, Iran is going to note that and strike.

FREEMAN: Kim, I'm curious, what do you expect U.S. retaliation to look like in the coming days? We've already seen eight straight nights of strikes against Iran.

DOZIER: Well, we've seen them expand. CENTCOM has been hitting bridges. And according to Iranian authorities, even desalination facilities. It seems the goal is now, you know, that they've run out of many of the conventional military targets to hit some of the economic targets, to make it harder for the government to maintain power. That gets them into the gray area of -- a lot of these things will be dual use.

When you take out a bridge, yes, U.S. military troops might go over that bridge, but also civilians use it, which has led to accusations across the Gulf and the Middle East. I just came back from Europe, where I heard this chorus from people that the U.S. was committing war crimes. That can't be great for the U.S. reputation long term.

And that is all to Iran's benefit, as Iran takes more pain than the U.S. can and continues to mete out at least reputational damage and some military damage as well.

DANNY FREEMAN, CNN ANCHOR: Kim, can you break down and help us understand, I mean, how vulnerable are some of these U.S. military bases in the region of the Middle East? Because, you know, I think that a lot of folks would have assumed that those would be a little bit more secure, certainly.

DOZIER (on-camera): Well, it's just like, you know, when I used to be based in Iraq, the U.S. bases were as secure as they could be. But we're undergoing pretty constant attack. That is happening right now in Iraq. Hundreds of attacks have happened against U.S. diplomatic and military facilities by militias who are funded by Iran, in some cases ordered by Iran.

So Iran has a lot of capability to harass U.S. troops, but also to by hitting the Gulf nations, which it's doing right now, it's increasing the pain for having those U.S. troops based in those countries, with the ultimate goal of getting the Gulf countries themselves to eject U.S. forces.

FREEMAN: Kim, from your perspective, does it seem like the administration continues to underestimate what Iran or its proxies are capable of?

DOZIER (on-camera): What I keep hearing out of the White House is, you know, the same kind of things I heard during the Iraq and the Afghan war, but we've destroyed so much. We've taken --

DOZIER (on-camera): -- so much equipment off of the battlefield. But what they don't seem to be able to do is take away the will to fight. And they are just confused and perplexed by that.

From Iran's perspective, though, as many of us who've watched Iran for a very long time said before these attacks by the U.S. and Israel even began, Iran is a society built on the strength of martyrdom and the strength of sacrifice. The more pain they endure, the better that is in terms of their support from the local population and also the admiration that they're increasingly getting from other countries around the globe.

And that spells everything from people willing to smuggle goods to Iran, to nation states like China and Russia, who are already Iranian allies, willing to do whatever they can to keep the Iranian government going, because remember, that's also tiring the U.S. out. And for an adversary like China, that's a good thing.

FREEMAN: Kim, last thing, and I know it's hard to assess this in still, I guess, relatively early days, but I mean, is this beginning to look like what forever wars look like?

DOZIER (on-camera): I think what we could be looking at is at least this tit-for-tat going through the end of this year. From the Iranian perspective, they don't want to hand Trump the diplomatic victory of a full sort of Iranian nuclear deal or anything approaching that. But they do need to get economic relief.

The recent ceasefire allowed them to get a lot of the oil that had backed up in all their storage facilities. It allowed them to get that oil out. It allowed them to get some cash in. So at least for a time, several weeks, I think you'll see this back and forth go on until Gulf countries will reach out and say, you know, there's got to be some peace talks because our people need relief from this constant barrage.

So I think we're going to see that seesaw back and forth as opposed to a forever war.

FREEMAN: Yes, again, especially with more of those U.S. allies being dragged in continuously.

Kim Dozier, thank you so much for your expertise as always. Good to see you.

DOZIER (on-camera): Thanks, Dan.

FREEMAN: All right, coming up, today's World Cup final will be a battle of the soccer giants. Argentina looking to repeat as champions, but Spain is standing in their way.

We're going to preview today's matchup coming up after a quick break.

And if you're heading out, remember, you can stream our show from anywhere in the U.S., right from the CNN app. You can also go to CNN.com/Watch. We're all over the place. See you in a bit.

FREEMAN: It all comes down to this. Argentina versus Spain this afternoon at New Jersey Stadium. Will Lionel Messi get that what could be his final World Cup trophy and be a back-to-back champion? Or will Spain pull off an upset?

Seth Vertelney with USA Today is here with us to talk through it all. Seth, it's been a pleasure having you with us on the weekends for this entire World Cup run. Great to see you. Let's start here.

What are you thinking? Argentina? Spain? Who's going to win?

SETH VERTELNEY, SOCCER EDITOR, USA TODAY: Oh, man, it should be a really good game. I'm really looking forward to this. If you had to ask me, I would probably make Spain a slight favorite. The reason is, Argentina, for all the quality that they have, has been really close in all four of their knockout games to losing. It's been sort of a high wire act with them. Even going back to the round of 32 against Cape Verde, they had to go into extra time and they continue to find ways to win in really dramatic fashion.

And, you know, we saw that again in the semifinal against England, they were trailing with about five minutes left and they found a way to score twice in the last six, seven minutes of that game to beat England and make it to the final. But this Spain team has been so solid throughout this entire World Cup. They have conceded one goal in seven games. And I think where I see Spain having an advantage is they're so good at keeping the ball. Their specialty is possession. And Argentina in the last five, 10, 20 minutes of that game against England had all of the ball.

And I think especially if Spain can get an early goal or can get a lead, it's going to be much harder for Argentina to come back today than it has been in previous games.

FREEMAN: No, that's super well said. But I guess that's the question, right? I mean, because Argentina, especially in the England game, have had these stunning last minute comeback wins, can Spain ever rest? Like even if they do get an early lead, what do they need to do to hold Argentina off from pulling another upset off?

VERTELNEY: Yes, I think they need to do what they do best, which is keep the ball. Spain, I think we saw it best exemplified against France in the semifinal. I mean, going into that game, I think people would make France a slight favorite because of how dynamic their attack had been throughout the tournament. In every game, it seemed like they were overwhelming teams.

And what Spain did is they put together a game plan where they were so defensively sound, their structure was so solid. And they really prevented France from getting a lot of openings on attack. And when Spain has the ball, they're able to maintain possession and they're able to find moments to hurt teams when they are in the attack in third.

And so I think that's what they need to do again today is have a really strong defensive structure, have a good shape, and they should feel confident, especially the way that they controlled the game against France. They should feel confident heading into today's game.

FREEMAN: Yes, because shutting down Mbappe is no easy feat.

Seth, are we thinking this is going to be Messi's final World Cup run?

VERTELNEY: I would imagine so. I mean, he turned 39 at this tournament. You know, you never want to say never, especially because in 2030, Argentina will host a game of this World Cup.

And so, you never know. Messi has been kind of taking things day by day, moment by moment for the last four years. You know, he never really said he was for sure going to play in

Return to Transcripts main page

U.S. Wraps Up 8th Night Of Strikes Against Iran; Kuwait Army Intercepting Missile, Drone Attacks; Court Documents Reveal ICE Office Connected To Fatal Maine Shooting Was Accused Of Abuse By Two Ex- Wives; Spain And Argentina Prepare To Face Off In World Cup Final; Flash Flooding Follows Days Of Smoke & Heat In New York. 2 U.S. Service Members Dead, 1 Missing After Friday Attack; Russia Launches Massive Missile and Drone Attack Against Ukraine; Jackson Takes Lead in Race to Replace Platner in Maine. Aired 7-8a ET

Aired July 19, 2026 - 07:00   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

DANNY FREEMAN, CNN HOST: Welcome back to CNN This Morning. Here's what you need to know today. First up, the U.S. launched another round of strikes on Iran Saturday in retaliation for the death of two U.S. soldiers. And happening now, we're learning of new missile attacks by Iran on U.S. allies. We've got that coming up in a live report.

And we're also following new escalations in the war between Russia and Ukraine. Russia launching a major ballistic missile attack on Kyiv. New information and video of the damage is coming in.

And we have more information about one of the officers connected to that deadly ICE shooting in Maine back on Monday. The new details CNN uncovered about his past coming up.

Plus, after more than 100 matches, thrilling wins and heartbreaking losses, it is all down to this, the World Cup final. Let's take a live look at the stadium where Spain and Argentina will face off later today. We're going to take you there live coming up.

Very good morning to you. It is Sunday, July 19th. Thank you so much for joining us. I'm Danny Freeman in for Victor Blackwell.

We begin with breaking news. The U.S. embassy in Jordan says the air and seaports in Aqaba are being evacuated. They're citing a, quote, "specific and credible threat."

Now, this right here happened just hours after U.S. Central Command posted this video. CENTCOM says it targeted Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps forces behind attacks that killed two U.S. service members in Jordan on Friday. One service member is still missing. Iranian state news says explosions were heard in Bandar Abbas and in the Strait of Hormuz.

CNN's Nic Robertson joins us now. Nic, what more are we learning this morning as this conflict intensifies?

NIC ROBERTSON, CNN INTERNATIONAL DIPLOMATIC EDITOR: Yes, this announcement by the Jordan -- excuse me -- by Jordanian officials that the U.S. Embassy in Jordan is also putting out about warning U.S. citizens not to go to the international airport or the seaport in Aqaba, which is right in the very south of Jordan on the Red Sea.

The Red Sea port there is is a new very new development. Specific and credible threat now just across the border into Israel from Aqaba is Eilat in Israel. And we know that Eilat in the past has been targeted by missiles that have been fired believed by the Houthis. Drones have been fired. Their Iran's missiles have also impacted just across the border in Israel in the earlier phase of the war back in March this year.

But it's not clear why Jordan is putting out these alerts about the airport and seaport in Aqaba. And it's worth noting as well that the Aqaba is quite some distance, significant distance from any of the air bases inside of Jordan that Iran says it's been targeting over the past week. So this is a significant development. It speaks to the broader warning that the State Department has put out for American citizens in this region.

At the moment, the Iranians, their government is saying or the state media in Iran is saying that the United States targeted one of their civilian nuclear facilities in the southwest of Iran, about 50, 80 miles over the border from Kuwait. So right in the southwest of Iran, Iranians are saying that the U.S. targeted that overnight.

We know that Qeshm Island, we know that Bandar Abbas in Iran were targets of U.S. Air Force overnight trying to get missile storage sites, drone storage sites, maritime sort of communication nodes along the Strait of Hormuz. But this morning as well, the Iranians targeting Kuwait again, targeting Bahrain again, the warning sirens going off in both of those countries.

So at the moment, this threat in Jordan, of course, that's the country where two U.S. service personnel, CENTCOM announced yesterday had been sadly killed in an Iranian strike at -- their one person, one service personnel. A person still missing in that attack. Four injured taken to hospital now released from hospital in Jordan. But it really does put Jordan very much in the crosshairs of Iran's actions against the U.S. interests in this region right now.

FREEMAN: Yes. Well said. All eyes on Jordan as this conflict again seems to be intensifying this weekend.

Nic Robertson, thank you very much. Appreciate your reporting.

Switching gears now, we have new details that are emerging about an ICE officer connected to the fatal shooting of a man in Maine. And we want to warn you, some of the video in this report may be disturbing. Now, CNN has confirmed that David Brouillette was among the officers at the scene. But CNN has not independently confirmed that Brouillette was the one who discharged his weapon.

CNN investigation has uncovered court records showing Brouillette previously reported lingering cognitive issues after a serious head injury and revealing other questions about his background. CNN's Gloria Pazmino takes a closer look.

GLORIA PAZMINO, CNN NATIONAL CORRESPONDENT: That's right. And we're getting a much fuller picture about who this officer is now that we have confirmed that David Brouillette was among the officers in the scene in Biddeford, Maine, where an ICE officer fatally shot, Joan Sebastian Duran Guerrero. This is all according to two of his ex-wives who reviewed photos and spoke with CNN.

Now, we should mention that CNN has not independently confirmed whether Brouillette was the officer who discharged the weapon killing Duran Guerrero. And investigators have not publicly identified the officer who shot the victim. Federal sources have separately confirmed to CNN that Brouillette is an ICE officer. The Department of Homeland Security has declined to comment on the identity of the officer, and they have said that the officer used deadly force because they feared for public safety.

Now, we have been trying to gather details to get a fuller picture about who Brouillette is. My colleagues Michael Williams, Kurt Devine and Priscilla Alvarez have been combing through public documents. And what has emerged is a troubling picture that raises serious questions about how ICE is doing its training and its officer vetting as they race to meet the demands of the Trump administration's immigration crackdown.

One of Brouillette's ex-wives and one of his daughters also told CNN that he contacted them days after the shooting and said that he had opened fire. Both said he told them that he thought the shooting was justified.

Now, here's some of what we have learned. In 2023, Brouillette told a court in Maine that he had suffered lingering cognitive issues after he suffered a serious head injury when a steel beam fell on his head during a training exercise for a firefighting certificate. He sued the Maine Community College system because of that injury, but it was just two years after he filed that lawsuit that he started working for ICE.

Brouillette's ex-wives also described a history of alleged domestic abuse and intimidation, all of this in court filings, where he denies the allegations. And we have not been able to confirm any criminal convictions or charges. We also reached out to Brouillette and he did not respond to several requests for comments.

We should mention Duran Guerrero's killing was the second time that an ICE officer shot and killed an immigrant behind the wheel in the span of just a week. And it's the fourth killing this year alone by federal immigration authorities.

Gloria Pazmino, CNN, New York.

FREEMAN: All right, Gloria, thank you for that reporting. All right. Still ahead, a brutal night across Ukraine as Russia unleashed its biggest missile attacks of the war. We have details ahead.

Plus, Taylor Farms is expanding its recall of iceberg linked to diarrhea outbreak. We'll tell you which products to look out for.

And let's take a live look now at East Rutherford, New Jersey, where just hours from now, Argentina and Spain are going to battle it out in the World Cup final. We're going to show you what to expect after a quick break.

FREEMAN: The time has come. After more than 100 matches, two teams are going to battle it out in today's FIFA World Cup final. Spain versus Argentina, and two soccer giants will meet on the pitch. Argentina's Lionel Messi versus Spain's Lamine Yamal.

Leigh Waldman is live outside of the New York-New Jersey stadium where these games are taking place. Leigh, the excitement is palpable. What are we expecting today?

LEIGH WALDMAN, CNN CORRESPONDENT: Danny, so much excitement in the air. We just heard them testing the mic system inside and, you know, it gets your heart pumping. We're ready to see these two teams face off.

And they play very different styles on the pitch, Spain and Argentina. People are comparing it to orchestra versus heavy metal. We know that Argentina will be very aggressive on the pitch.

Meanwhile, Spain is hoping to play a complex passing game and take any advantage that they can to try and score on Argentina to make this match even more special. It's the very first time in a World Cup final we are seeing it contested by the champions of the world versus the champions of England. So many fans coming here trying to take in a moment out of what is going to be an incredible match here, Danny.

FREEMAN: I'm curious, I know that President Trump is expected to attend today's game. Can you take us through what that means for security for this final?

WALDMAN: Absolutely. So we have heard from FIFA officials and they say they are heavily increasing the security measures that are in place here at the stadium behind us. Not only because of President Donald Trump, but multiple other international dignitaries who are expected to be in attendance today.

This is a SEAR 1 event. That is the highest level of security coordinated by the FBI, DHS and Secret Service. They're right now are putting in active roadblocks to shut down the traffic around this stadium. Fans who are in attendance today, they can expect to go through extensive TSA style screenings in order to get inside of this stadium.

It's a no drone zone. If you're coming to this game today, you're being told come hours early ahead of that 3:00 p.m. kickoff.

FREEMAN: Wow, that's wild. Meanwhile, Leigh, New York I know has been dealing with a lot of poor air quality conditions from those Canadian wildfires. We were looking at a live picture of the stadium a moment ago.

Still seems a little hazy out there. How is it out there? Is it going to affect the game today? What do you think?

WALDMAN: Just a little bit hazy. But, Danny, we never cheer for weekend rain showers, right? Yesterday was a rainy day in New York City. But that's actually helping with the wildfire smoke that we've been dealing with these last few days.

Last week, very hazy, smelled like a campfire all throughout New York City. Right now, this is a lot better. It's going to be humid. There's still going to be a little bit of smoke in the air. But 80 degrees today when these two teams kick off. So it's going to be a much better day for the players on the pitch but also for the fans who are here to attend.

FREEMAN: All right. Last thing, Leigh. We can't talk about the final without talking about ticket prices. How much is it going to cost for last-minute tickets? Should we get one? Should we get two tickets and go?

WALDMAN: OK, Danny. So I'm going to say our DMs are open. So if anyone is feeling generous and wants to send us some tickets, we can go together. But unless you've been saving up probably your entire life, I don't think you're affording a ticket inside today.

The cheapest tickets, Joey, my producer, and I were looking this morning, almost $8,000. And that's for a nosebleed seat. If you want to get closer to the action, you're shelling out $20,000, $30,000, even $40,000 for one of these tickets. But they're also offering some bundle packages to include your food and drinks.

Who doesn't want that? $32,000 to $33,000 there. This is an expensive game to go ahead and see. All those fans who are flying into the United States, I don't know where they're getting all this money, man.

FREEMAN: This just in. The Jordanian government is now denying there were evacuations of air or sea ports there. And this comes after the U.S. embassy said they were evacuated due to a credible threat.

The U.S. has now bombed Iran for an eighth straight night. Now targeting facilities for air and coastal defenses and weapons storage sites, according to U.S. Central Command. And two American soldiers were killed following Iranian strikes in Jordan Friday and one is still missing.

Joining me now is Major John Spencer, chair of War Studies for the Madison Policy Forum. He's also the author of the upcoming book, "20 Times I Almost Died." Major Spencer, thank you so much for joining us this morning on a hard weekend for our service members.

Let's start there, though. We still don't have a lot of information about these attacks in Jordan that killed those two service members. But what's your perspective here? What do you think this means for next steps in this war?

MAJOR JOHN SPENCER (RET,), CHAIR OF WAR STUDIES, MADISON POLICY FORUM: I think that it means that, one, we're going to mourn our losses but understand that the greatest thing we can do, in my opinion, to our service members is to accomplish the mission and finish the job. We have an integrated air defense system on all of our bases, but Iran's continuing to attack all of the countries, I think seven countries in seven days, where U.S. forces or not, because they're attacking desalination plants in Kuwait and other locations, striking back at the U.S. attacks now on eight consecutive nights.

FREEMAN: What do you believe this says about the vulnerability of our troops or our military installations in the region right now?

SPENCER: I mean, I think we have very strong defenses, but there is no perfect system, even what we've seen in Israel that has, you know, its Iron Dome and other systems. If you can still get about 1 percent, and that's what Iran is getting, depending on where we're talking about, that's still something's going to get through and adjustments are made, and they've been using different types of tactics, you know, different missiles of the capabilities they have left.

FREEMAN: Major, I'm curious, from your perspective, is the U.S. right now underestimating the capabilities of Iran and these proxies in the region? Because we've been told time and time again that their military has been seriously weakened, and yet we see instances like this.

SPENCER: No, I don't think we underestimate them, the U.S. military, and I think we're moving more assets into the region to stop their ability to influence the Strait of Hormuz. That's what we're really seeing now is the Battle of Hormuz being fought through and U.S. forces in different areas of these countries. Of course, they're in harm's way, right? That's what our troops are signed up for, but I do think we're underestimating them. And even if you destroyed a vast majority of their capabilities, they still have capabilities. It's a large country that was invested in these capabilities, drones, missiles, for a long time to be able to do just this.

So I don't think it's underestimating. I mean, it's the will that is the problem at this point, not the capability, although we don't like seeing any capability being projected out of Iran, but that's the importance of the mission is that this is about changing the regime's behavior, not a regime change operation, and they're vastly different.

FREEMAN: Major, from your view, are we closer to peace today? I mean, it seemed like for a moment there, there were signings of MOUs, there were steps towards ceasefires, and then it seems like in the past week plus or so, certainly this weekend, we've taken several steps backwards.

SPENCER: Yes, I think we all had a -- or had a hope that the memorandum of understanding, which is an agreement to have a conversation, would lead to a negotiated settlement of the operation. I mean, I don't think we're closer now, of course.

According to President Trump, the ceasefire is over. The MOU, despite both sides maybe thinking that it said something different in -- especially in relation to the Strait of Hormuz, I don't think we're closer to peace at this moment and that we will likely have to accelerate the operation in order to achieve that goal of getting to the negotiation table.

It's always going to end at a negotiation table, but I do believe that the region and the United States is safer today. They're not closer to a nuclear weapon. Their capabilities to project power have been drastically impacted.

FREEMAN: Major, the Pentagon has mostly stopped doing media briefings now and updates regularly on the war, so reporters have not really been able to ask questions of the administration about the latest objectives or where things stand. I'm just curious, what do you make of that? Do you think that's an important thing that should be happening more frequently?

SPENCER: Absolutely. I'm definitely a big fan of communicating at all levels, so both from the White House Press Corps Secretary and the Pentagon. But I think we're in this situation where they do want the negotiations to continue, and according to President Trump and others, that they are talking to people in Iran, and people want that to work without the statements, even though the new supreme leader made a statement, although nobody's seen him, on what he thinks about the negotiation and memorandum understanding. I think we're in a sensitive situation, and hopefully we do see those briefings, because I do think they're a daily to have them, is important.

DANNY FREEMAN, CNN ANCHOR: And last question for me, Major, is, I know it's hard to project into the future, especially since we're only still a few months into this conflict, but is this looking more and more like a forever war to you?

SPENCER: No, not at all. And I use that term very, I don't use that often, because I understand this isn't a forever war, a nation- building war, we're going to overthrow the regime and institute a democracy. This is a limited war. Four limited objectives, I think, are worth fighting for, in my opinion, non-nuclear proliferation and the freedom of navigation, which would impact the U.S. economy from the gas prices to fertilizer across the globe if Iran was allowed to do what they are trying to say that they want to do, which is control the Strait of Hormuz.

I think it's worth fighting for and achieving this limited goal, but not a forever war. And although we are, you know, whether you count the entire time, five months into it, that there has been progress made. And I do believe that there are people willing to come to terms, but it has to be on these very non-negotiable aspects of no nuclear weapons and freedom of navigation.

FREEMAN: Major John Spencer, thank you very much for your insight and perspective this morning. Really do appreciate it.

SPENCER: Thanks, Danny.
